title: Listening - Part 4: Running an Independent Bookshop
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a woman called Chloe, who owns an independent bookshop. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: My guest today is Chloe Hughes, who owns an independent bookshop. Chloe, you previously worked in publishing. Why open a shop? || Chloe: Publishing taught me a lot, but most of my work involved emails and sales figures. [24]I missed talking directly to readers and finding out why a particular book mattered to them[/24]. A bookshop gave me that conversation every day, although I knew it would also mean ordering stock, cleaning shelves and worrying about bills. || Presenter: How did you choose the location? || Chloe: I looked at fashionable streets first, but the rents were impossible. Then I found a smaller empty shop opposite the public library. [25]I chose it because people already came to that part of town looking for books, even though it wasn’t the busiest shopping area[/25]. It turned out to be a good decision. || Presenter: Do you stock only books you personally admire? || Chloe: That would be enjoyable for about a week, then the business would fail. I need to know what customers want. [26]I reserve one display table for books I’m enthusiastic about, while the rest of the shop reflects what local readers actually ask for[/26]. Sometimes a customer introduces me to an author I’ve never read. || Presenter: You hold evening events too. How did the first one go? || Chloe: I invited a novelist from our county and arranged plenty of chairs. Before the event, I feared nobody would appear. Then forty people came, but I still worried they’d sit silently. [27]Once questions began, the audience discussed the book so energetically that I had to end the evening later than planned[/27]. It showed me readers want to meet one another. || Presenter: Is selling online important for you? || Chloe: I can’t compete with huge websites on price or delivery speed. During the pandemic I created an ordering page, and I’ve kept it, but [28]the service customers value most is my ability to recommend a book after a short conversation[/28]. That’s something a list of automated suggestions cannot easily replace. || Presenter: Which part of the shop has developed unexpectedly? || Chloe: The children’s corner. I thought parents would simply buy popular titles. Instead, [29]local primary schools began visiting regularly, and the demand for children’s books has more than doubled[/29]. We now have story sessions on Saturday mornings, which are noisy but wonderful. || Presenter: What advice would you give someone dreaming of a bookshop? || Chloe: Loving books is important, but it doesn’t pay rent by itself. [30]Before opening, make sure you have enough money to survive a slow first year while customers discover you[/30]. It takes time for a shop to become part of a community. || Presenter: Chloe, thank you. || Chloe: Thank you.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: Why did Chloe decide to open a bookshop?

options.A: She wanted direct contact with people who read books.
options.B: She was unable to find work in publishing.
options.C: She wanted to write about books herself.

questions.explanation: Chloe missed speaking directly to readers about what books meant to them.

questions.question: Why did Chloe choose her shop's location?

options.A: The rent was lower than anywhere else in town.
options.B: It was an area already used by people interested in books.
options.C: It was in the town's busiest shopping street.

questions.explanation: She chose a shop opposite the public library because people already visited the area for books.

questions.question: How does Chloe decide which books to stock?

options.A: She mainly follows recommendations from publishers.
options.B: She limits her stock to books she has enjoyed herself.
options.C: She combines her own choices with local demand.

questions.explanation: One table features her own recommendations, while most stock reflects readers' requests.

questions.question: What surprised Chloe about her first author event?

options.A: The audience became very involved in discussion.
options.B: People arrived without having read the book.
options.C: The author stayed for less time than expected.

questions.explanation: The audience discussed the novel so enthusiastically that the event ran late.

questions.question: What does Chloe believe customers most appreciate?

options.A: Receiving books quickly through her website.
options.B: Obtaining personal recommendations in the shop.
options.C: Paying lower prices than they find online.

questions.explanation: She says customers value her ability to recommend a book after talking to them.

questions.question: What has happened in the children's section?

options.A: It has become more popular through school involvement.
options.B: It has needed to reduce its range of popular titles.
options.C: It has been moved to make space for regular events.

questions.explanation: School visits have helped more than double demand for children's books.

questions.question: What does Chloe advise future bookshop owners to do?

options.A: Develop an online store before renting a shop.
options.B: Offer activities for children from the beginning.
options.C: Be financially prepared for a gradual start.

questions.explanation: She recommends having enough money to manage while the business slowly attracts customers.
